topics.overview:
Karel the Robot is CS106A’s entry point into programming, offering a gentle, visual way to learn fundamental ideas such as sequencing, control flow, decomposition, and debugging. In a grid world of streets (rows) and avenues (columns), Karel follows simple commands—move, turn, and manipulate beepers—while responding to conditions like walls or the presence of beepers. This setting strips away distractions and emphasizes algorithmic thinking: breaking problems into steps, choosing appropriate control structures, and reasoning about preconditions and postconditions.

Within a few assignments, students progress from writing straight-line code to crafting reusable helper methods, mastering while/if logic, and recognizing patterns like the fencepost problem. Karel models best practices used later in full Java programs—abstraction, top-down design, and careful testing—making the transition to general Java programming (graphics, data, and beyond) both natural and confident.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
Karel’s world is a 2D grid with walls and beepers. Karel executes primitive commands (move, turnLeft, putBeeper, pickBeeper) and can query the environment with boolean tests (frontIsClear, beepersPresent, etc.). Students learn to compose these basics with control structures—sequence, conditionals (if/else), and loops (while/for)—to create robust behaviors.

Decomposition is central: large tasks are split into clear helper methods with documented preconditions (what must be true before calling) and postconditions (what will be true after). Students practice top-down design, defining methods like moveToWall, placeBeepersOnRow, or climbHurdle, each with a single, well-specified responsibility. Common algorithmic pitfalls (e.g., the fencepost bug) are introduced alongside strategies to avoid them (e.g., place-on-entry vs. place-on-exit patterns), reinforcing disciplined problem solving and testing.

By the end, learners not only command Karel effectively but also internalize core programming habits—abstraction, decomposition, invariants, and defensive checks—that directly generalize to standard Java programming.
learningObjectives: Write Karel programs that correctly navigate a world using primitive commands and tests.
Apply control flow (if/else, while) to implement repeated and conditional behavior.
Design helper methods with clear preconditions and postconditions to decompose complex tasks.
Identify and avoid fencepost and off-by-one errors in traversal patterns.
Reason about Karel’s state (position, direction, beeper bag) to predict and debug program behavior.
commonMistakes: Using while(frontIsClear()) with the wrong action order, causing a fencepost bug (missing the first or last corner).
Assuming turnRight() exists in base Karel (it does not—use SuperKarel or implement via three left turns).
Forgetting to test conditions before moving, leading to runtime errors when Karel runs into walls.
tipsForSuccess: State preconditions/postconditions for each helper method and verify them at call sites.
Trace code mentally (or on paper) corner-by-corner before running to catch logic errors early.
Build incrementally: implement and test one small behavior at a time before combining them.

quiz.question: Which command moves Karel forward by exactly one corner in the direction it is facing?
quiz.explanation: move() advances Karel one corner; the others rotate or manipulate beepers.

quiz.question: What happens if Karel executes move() when there is a wall directly ahead?
options: A) Karel climbs over the wall
B) Karel crashes/throws an error and stops
C) Karel waits until the wall disappears
D) Karel removes the wall and moves
quiz.explanation: Moving into a wall causes a runtime error; guard moves with frontIsClear().

quiz.question: Which condition checks whether a beeper is on Karel’s current corner?
quiz.explanation: beepersPresent() tests beepers at the current corner; beepersInBag() is about Karel’s inventory.

quiz.question: Choose the best description of while vs. if in Karel programs.
options: A) if runs repeatedly; while runs once
B) Both run repeatedly as long as the condition is true
C) if runs at most once; while repeats while the condition is true
quiz.explanation: An if executes the body once at most; a while re-evaluates the condition and may run many times.

quiz.question: In base Karel (not SuperKarel), which helper correctly defines turnRight()?
quiz.explanation: Three left turns equal one right turn.

quiz.question: Consider: while (frontIsClear()) { putBeeper(); move(); } starting on the first corner of a row. Which is true?
options: A) The last corner will be left without a beeper (fencepost bug)
B) The first corner is skipped
C) Two beepers end up on the last corner
D) It places a beeper on every corner correctly
quiz.explanation: The loop stops before the last corner because frontIsClear() becomes false; the last corner never gets a beeper.

quiz.question: Which loop moves Karel from its current corner straight to the wall ahead (without crashing)?
quiz.explanation: Repeatedly moving while the path is clear reaches the wall safely.

quiz.question: Which statement about SuperKarel is true?
options: A) Base Karel already has turnRight()
B) SuperKarel adds helpers like turnRight() and turnAround()
C) putBeeper() exists only in SuperKarel
quiz.explanation: SuperKarel extends Karel with convenience methods such as turnRight() and turnAround().

quiz.question: A good postcondition for a helper method moveToWall() is:
options: A) Karel is at the origin
C) Karel is facing the same direction with a wall immediately ahead
D) Karel has placed a beeper
quiz.explanation: moveToWall() typically preserves direction and stops with frontIsBlocked().

quiz.question: Given: if (frontIsClear()) { move(); } else { turnLeft(); } and a wall is ahead, what happens?
options: B) Karel moves through the wall
quiz.explanation: The condition is false, so the else branch executes and Karel turns left.

syntaxReference:
Helper methods (decomposition): 
private void moveToWall() { while (frontIsClear()) { move(); } }

Implementing turnRight in base Karel: 
private void turnRight() { turnLeft(); turnLeft(); turnLeft(); }
commonPatterns:
Move to wall safely: 
while (frontIsClear()) { move(); } // stop when frontIsBlocked()

Conditional action each step: 
while (frontIsClear()) { if (beepersPresent()) { pickBeeper(); } move(); }

Stair/hurdle climb (SuperKarel): 
turnLeft(); move(); turnRight(); move(); turnRight(); move(); turnLeft();

Check-last-corner cleanup: 
// For actions that must include the last corner, do the action after each move OR place before entering loop as needed.
quickTips: Guard moves with frontIsClear() to avoid crashes.
Write small helpers for repeated sequences (e.g., turnRight, moveToWall).
State pre/postconditions for every helper and preserve them during refactors.

topics.overview:
Control flow is how a program decides what to do next—whether to run code in sequence, make a decision, or repeat an action. In CS106A, you move from writing straight-line code to programs that react to conditions and iterate over data. You will practice reading input, branching with if/else and switch, and looping with while, for, and do-while. These constructs let you express algorithms like input validation, searching, counting, and pattern generation.

Beyond learning syntax, you will develop fluency with boolean logic (relational and logical operators), nesting, and common loop patterns (counter-controlled, sentinel-controlled, and fencepost problems). You will also learn to reason about loop execution counts, avoid off-by-one errors, and write clear, structured code that is easy to test and debug.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
Control flow determines the order in which statements execute. The three pillars are sequence (default top-to-bottom execution), selection (if/else, else-if chains, switch) to choose among alternatives, and iteration (while, for, do-while) to repeat work. Conditions are boolean expressions built from relational operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=) and logical operators (&&, ||, !). Java uses short-circuit evaluation for && and ||, which can prevent unnecessary work and runtime errors (e.g., division by zero) when used carefully.

Loops appear in several patterns. Counter-controlled loops (for) run a known number of times; sentinel-controlled loops (while) process input until a special value is encountered; input-validation loops (while) keep prompting until a condition is satisfied. Nested loops let you work in grids and generate patterns. Key pitfalls include off-by-one errors, incorrect loop conditions, and misusing == with Strings (use equals). Use braces consistently, leverage descriptive conditions, and test with boundary cases to ensure correctness.
learningObjectives: Use if/else, else-if, and switch to implement multi-way decisions.
Write while, for, and do-while loops to implement counter-controlled and sentinel-controlled iteration.
Construct and evaluate boolean expressions with correct operator precedence and short-circuiting.
Design nested loops to generate 2D patterns and compute aggregate results.
Debug and prevent common control-flow errors such as off-by-one, infinite loops, and incorrect string comparisons.
commonMistakes: Using == to compare Strings instead of calling equals, which causes unexpected false results.
Writing loop conditions that never become false (infinite loops) or are off by one (<= vs <).
Forgetting break statements in switch cases, unintentionally falling through to later cases.
tipsForSuccess: Trace your code by hand with small inputs and boundary values to confirm conditions and loop counts.
Name boolean variables and methods clearly (e.g., isValid, hasNext) to make conditions readable.
Exploit short-circuiting to guard risky operations (first check non-null or non-zero, then compute).

quiz.question: What is the value of boolean b after: boolean b = true || false && false;
quiz.explanation: && has higher precedence than ||, so expression is true || (false && false) => true || false => true.

quiz.question: Given: int x = 5; String s; if (x > 5) { s = "A"; } else if (x == 5) { s = "B"; } else { s = "C"; } What is s?
quiz.explanation: x equals 5, so the else-if branch runs and assigns "B".

quiz.question: How many times does count increment? int count = 0; for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { for (int j = 0; j < 2; j++) { count++; } }
quiz.explanation: Outer loop runs 3 times; inner runs 2 times each: 3*2 = 6 increments.

quiz.question: What is printed? int n = 2; String s = ""; switch (n) { case 1: s += "a"; case 2: s += "b"; break; case 3: s += "c"; } System.out.print(s);
quiz.explanation: Execution jumps to case 2 and appends "b" then breaks. No fall-through from case 1 occurs.

quiz.question: Which loop guarantees the body executes at least once?
quiz.explanation: do-while evaluates its condition after the first iteration.

quiz.question: What happens? int x = 0; if (x != 0 && 10 / x > 1) { System.out.println("yes"); }
options: C) No output; no exception due to short-circuit
quiz.explanation: x != 0 is false; with && the RHS is not evaluated, so no exception and nothing prints.

quiz.question: How many numbers print? for (int i = 0; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.print(i + " "); }
quiz.explanation: i takes values 0 through 5 inclusive, which is 6 numbers.

quiz.question: Using -1 as a sentinel, what sum is computed? int sum = 0; int val; // Input: 3 4 -1 5 while ((val = sc.nextInt()) != -1) { sum += val; } System.out.print(sum);
quiz.explanation: Only 3 and 4 are added before encountering -1. The 5 after -1 is not read.

quiz.question: Which condition avoids NullPointerException if s may be null when checking for "yes"?
quiz.explanation: "yes".equals(s) is safe when s is null; calling s.equals would throw a NullPointerException.

quiz.question: What is printed? for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) { if (i % 2 == 0) continue; System.out.print(i); }
quiz.explanation: continue skips even i values, so prints 1 and 3.

Prefer for loops when the number of iterations is known; use while for open-ended processes.

topics.overview:
Decomposition is the practice of breaking a large programming problem into smaller, focused pieces. In CS106A, this usually means designing a program as a collection of short, single-purpose methods and, later, grouping related methods and data into classes. By separating concerns, you reduce complexity, make your code easier to reason about, test, and debug, and enable reuse of components across different parts of a program or even different assignments.

In a typical CS106A workflow, you use top-down design and stepwise refinement: start with a clear statement of the problem, outline high-level steps as method calls, then iteratively refine each method until it is straightforward to implement. Along the way, you define clear method signatures, document preconditions and postconditions, keep methods cohesive (each does one thing well), and minimize coupling (methods know as little as necessary about each other). This approach helps you produce clean, readable, and maintainable Java code.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary: Decomposition is central to programming methodology: you divide a complex task into a hierarchy of small, well-named, and well-scoped methods. Start with top-down design—write an outline that expresses the solution as a sequence of high-level steps, each encapsulated in a helper method. Then apply stepwise refinement—expand each helper method into simpler sub-steps until each method is easy to implement and test. Strong decomposition relies on clear method signatures (name, parameters, return type), explicit contracts (preconditions and postconditions), and purposeful naming that reveals intent. Good methods have high cohesion (do one thing) and participate in a design with low coupling (minimal knowledge of other parts). Use constants to avoid magic numbers, pass only the data a method needs, and return results rather than relying on global state. Early in development, write stubs (temporary method bodies) to compile and establish structure; then incrementally replace stubs with real logic and test each part in isolation. As you progress toward object-oriented design, you further decompose by grouping related state and behavior into classes with clear interfaces, preserving the same principles of abstraction, cohesion, and low coupling.
learningObjectives: Apply top-down design to outline a program as a set of high-level helper methods.
Design clear method signatures with appropriate parameters and return types.
Document and honor method preconditions and postconditions to define contracts.
Refactor long or repetitive code into small, cohesive, reusable helper methods.
Use stubs and incremental testing to develop decomposed solutions reliably.
commonMistakes: Writing a single large method that does everything instead of separating responsibilities.
Leaking unnecessary details between methods (high coupling) by sharing too much state or using global variables.
Choosing poor method names or signatures that obscure intent, lack needed parameters, or return the wrong information.
tipsForSuccess: Draft the high-level algorithm as method calls first; fill in method bodies later.
Aim for methods under ~20–30 lines that each answer a single, well-named question.
Write small tests for helpers as you go; refactor early when you see duplication.

quiz.question: Which is the primary benefit of decomposition in CS106A?
options: A) It makes code run faster by default
B) It reduces complexity and improves readability/testability
C) It eliminates all bugs automatically
D) It avoids the need for comments entirely
quiz.explanation: Decomposition mainly improves design quality: readability, modularity, and testability. It does not inherently guarantee speed or eliminate all bugs.

quiz.question: Which method signature best follows good decomposition for checking evenness?
quiz.explanation: The method needs input (n) and returns a boolean result, so boolean isEven(int n) is appropriate.

quiz.question: A precondition is best described as:
options: A) A guarantee after a method executes
B) A requirement before calling a method
C) The same as a return value
quiz.explanation: Preconditions are requirements on inputs/state that must hold when a method is called.

quiz.question: Which code smell suggests poor decomposition?
options: A) Multiple short helper methods with clear names
B) A single 150-line method doing input, processing, and output
C) Using constants for configuration values
quiz.explanation: An overly long, multi-purpose method indicates low cohesion and high complexity.

quiz.question: You have methods readData(), computeStats(), and printReport(). What design principle is most improved?
quiz.explanation: Each method handles one focused responsibility, increasing cohesion.

quiz.question: Which option minimizes coupling between methods?
options: A) Accessing and modifying global variables in helpers
B) Passing only needed values as parameters and returning results
C) Using many public fields across classes
D) Performing I/O inside every helper
quiz.explanation: Passing required data and returning results reduces shared state and interdependencies.

quiz.question: Given: // requires: arr.length > 0 // ensures: returns max element. Which is best?
quiz.explanation: The method returns the maximum as an int; it needs the array as input.

quiz.question: When starting a large assignment, why write method stubs first?
options: B) They allow code to compile and support incremental development/testing
C) They eliminate the need for unit tests
quiz.explanation: Stubs let you compile and integrate structure early, then fill in logic incrementally.

quiz.question: Which redesign best increases cohesion?
options: A) Method process() handles input, parse, compute, print
B) Split into readInput(), parse(), compute(), print()
C) Move all code into main()
D) Replace parameters with global variables
quiz.explanation: Separating responsibilities into distinct methods increases cohesion and clarity.

quiz.question: You have two helpers: normalize(String s) and tokenize(String s). A new feature needs case-insensitive tokenization. What change keeps low coupling?
options: A) Have tokenize() read from System.in directly
B) Store s in a global and let both helpers access it
C) Call normalize() first, then pass its result to tokenize()
D) Merge both into one large method
quiz.explanation: Compose helpers by passing outputs to inputs; avoid global state and monolithic methods.

pseudocodeExamples.title: Reusable Helpers: Password Validation
pseudocodeExamples.description: Shows decomposing validation into small, testable checks that combine into a final decision.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
isValidPassword(pw):
  return isLongEnough(pw) AND hasUpper(pw) AND hasDigit(pw)

isLongEnough(pw):
  return length(pw) >= 8

hasUpper(pw):
  for each char c in pw:
    if isUppercase(c): return true
  return false

hasDigit(pw):
  for each char c in pw:
    if isDigit(c): return true
  return false

pseudocodeExamples.title: Refactoring Loop Logic: Prime Listing
pseudocodeExamples.description: Illustrates extracting helpers from a loop-heavy task to increase readability and testability.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
main:
  n <- readUpperBound()
  for i from 2 to n:
    if isPrime(i):
      print i

readUpperBound:
  read int from user
  return it

isPrime(k):
  if k < 2: return false
  for d from 2 to floor(sqrt(k)):
    if k % d == 0: return false
  return true

topics.overview:
In CS106A, variables are the named storage locations you use to keep track of information while your program runs. Each variable has a type that determines what kind of data it can store and which operations are valid on that data. Java distinguishes between primitive types (like int, double, boolean, and char) that store simple values directly, and reference types (like String and arrays) that store references to objects in memory. You will learn how to declare variables, assign and reassign values, use literals, and apply operators to compute results.

A core part of programming fluency is understanding how type affects behavior—especially differences between integer and floating-point arithmetic, how characters and strings work, and when explicit casts are needed to convert between types. You will also practice using final constants to capture fixed values clearly, choosing meaningful variable names, and reading compiler error messages about incompatible types. Mastering these fundamentals sets you up to write clear, correct programs throughout the course.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
A variable is a named container with a declared type that determines what values it can hold and what operations are allowed. Java is statically typed, meaning the type of each variable is checked at compile time and does not change. Primitive types (int, double, boolean, char) store raw values; reference types (e.g., String) store references to objects. Declarations introduce a variable and its type; initialization assigns the first value; assignment later updates it. Literals represent fixed values in code, like 42, 3.14, 'A', true, or "hello".

Arithmetic on ints uses integer division (3 / 2 == 1), while doubles produce floating-point results (3.0 / 2 == 1.5). Casting converts between compatible types: a narrowing cast like (int) 3.9 truncates toward zero, while widening (int to double) happens automatically. Characters are 16-bit Unicode values; you can do arithmetic with chars because they are numeric under the hood. Strings are immutable reference types; operations like concatenation produce new String objects. Use final to create constants (final double TAX_RATE = 0.095;) that cannot be reassigned. Clear naming, correct types, and careful use of casting help prevent subtle bugs.
learningObjectives: Declare, initialize, and assign variables of primitive and reference types in Java
Predict the results and types of expressions involving int and double, including integer division and modulo
Use explicit casting to control type conversion and avoid unintended truncation or precision loss
Explain the difference between primitive and reference types, including String immutability
Define and use final constants to improve clarity and maintainability
commonMistakes: Expecting 3 / 2 to be 1.5 instead of 1 due to integer division
Forgetting that Strings are immutable and assuming s.toUpperCase() changes s in place
Casting in the wrong place, e.g., (double)(sum / count) instead of ((double) sum) / count
tipsForSuccess: Choose types deliberately; when you need fractional results, use double or cast before dividing
Use final constants for magic numbers to self-document your code and prevent accidental changes
Read compiler error messages about incompatible types carefully—they often tell you exactly what to fix

topics.overview:
In CS106A, loops are the backbone of repetition: they let your program perform the same action many times without duplicating code. You will learn when to choose a for, while, or do-while loop; how to control iterations with counters and conditions; and how to process collections using the enhanced for loop. Loops power tasks like summing values, validating input, drawing graphics repeatedly (in ACM libraries), and traversing arrays or strings.

We distinguish between definite loops (a known number of iterations) and indefinite loops (repeat until a condition or sentinel appears). You will practice structuring loop state with counters, accumulators, and flags; avoiding off-by-one and infinite loops; and reasoning about nested loops for 2D patterns. Mastery includes understanding loop invariants, variable scope within loop headers and bodies, and the proper use of break and continue.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
A loop repeatedly executes a block while a condition holds (while), for a fixed progression (for), or at least once before checking (do-while). Use for when you know the iteration count or have a simple counter pattern; use while for open-ended repetition controlled by a condition or sentinel; use do-while for at-least-once input validation or menus. The enhanced for loop (for-each) iterates through arrays/collections without explicit indexing.

Core mechanics: a loop has initialization (set up counters/state), a condition (tested before or after each iteration), a body (work per iteration), and an update (change state to move toward termination). Typical variables include a counter (tracks iterations), an accumulator (aggregates results), and sometimes a sentinel (special value to end input). Avoid off-by-one errors by being precise about inclusive/exclusive bounds and indices (0..n-1 for arrays). Prevent infinite loops by ensuring the condition eventually becomes false. Nested loops multiply work across two dimensions (rows/columns) and require careful control of inner vs. outer indices. Use break to exit early, continue to skip to the next iteration, and be mindful of scope: loop variables declared in headers are not visible outside.

Reasoning tools: state diagrams and loop invariants help you prove correctness—identify what must remain true before and after each iteration. Prefer clear loop conditions over cleverness, and test edge cases (empty input, single element, n=0) to catch boundary bugs.
learningObjectives: Choose the appropriate loop construct (for, while, do-while, enhanced for) for a given problem.
Write counter-controlled and sentinel-controlled loops that terminate correctly.
Use accumulators, counters, and flags to maintain loop state and compute aggregates.
Trace and debug loops, identifying off-by-one and infinite loop errors.
Design and analyze nested loops to generate patterns or traverse 2D data.
commonMistakes: Using <= instead of < (or vice versa) and indexing past array bounds.
Forgetting to update the loop variable or state, causing infinite loops.
Modifying a collection while using enhanced for, leading to ConcurrentModificationException (when using Java collections).
tipsForSuccess: Write the loop header last: first write the goal as comments, define what changes each iteration, then encode init/condition/update.
Test with edge cases (n=0, n=1, smallest/ largest inputs) and print intermediate values while debugging.
Keep loop bodies small; extract helper methods for clarity and easier reasoning about invariants.

vocabulary.definition: A definite loop with initialization, condition, and update in one header; ideal for counter-controlled repetition.
An indefinite loop that repeats while a boolean condition remains true; condition checked before each iteration.
A loop that executes the body at least once, then repeats while the condition remains true.

vocabulary.definition: A special value signaling the end of input or termination of a loop.

The block of statements executed each iteration.

quiz.question: Which loop construct guarantees the body runs at least once?

Which option correctly prints each element of int[] arr = {2,4,6};?

C) for (int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) { System.out.println(arr[i]); }

quiz.question: What does continue do inside a loop?

quiz.explanation: i is scoped to the for loop header/body and not visible after the loop.

topics.overview:
Conditions let your program make decisions by evaluating boolean expressions that are either true or false. In CS106A, you use conditions to branch control flow with if, else-if, and else blocks, enabling different behavior based on user input, sensor data, or computed values. You’ll learn to combine comparisons (like <, <=, >, >=, ==, !=) with logical operators (&&, ||, !) to express complex rules clearly and correctly.

Beyond basic syntax, you’ll practice reasoning about order of evaluation and short-circuit behavior, which affect performance and correctness (e.g., checking for null before accessing an object). You’ll compare Strings properly with equals, avoid pitfalls with floating-point comparisons, and write readable chains of conditions for classification tasks. Mastery of conditions is essential for input validation, guard clauses, robust error handling, and laying the groundwork for loops and algorithms later in the course.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
A condition is a boolean expression whose value controls program flow. Core building blocks include relational operators (<, <=, >, >=), equality/inequality (==, !=) for primitives, and logical operators (&&, ||, !). Java evaluates && and || with short-circuit semantics: the right-hand side is evaluated only if needed, which you can use to guard against NullPointerException (e.g., s != null && s.length() > 0). Precedence rules place ! above && above ||; parentheses make intent explicit and prevent bugs.

Use if, if-else, and else-if chains to select among alternatives; prefer mutually exclusive, ordered checks for ranges. Nesting ifs is sometimes necessary but can often be replaced with clearer combined conditions. For multi-way selection on a single expression (typically integral, enum, or string), switch can be more readable than long else-if chains. Compare Strings with equals or equalsIgnoreCase, not ==. When negating complex conditions, apply De Morgan's laws to maintain correctness. For readability and safety, use guard clauses (early returns) to handle invalid states before the main logic.
learningObjectives: Write correct if, if-else, and else-if chains to implement branching logic
Combine relational and logical operators to express complex boolean conditions
Apply short-circuit evaluation to write safe null checks and guard clauses
Choose correct comparison methods for primitives vs. objects (e.g., equals for String)
Refactor nested or verbose conditions into clear, maintainable code using patterns like range checks and De Morgan's laws
commonMistakes: Using == to compare Strings instead of equals, causing unexpected false results
Incorrect range ordering in else-if chains (e.g., checking lower thresholds before higher ones and shadowing later cases)
Forgetting short-circuit or null checks before dereferencing objects, leading to NullPointerException
tipsForSuccess: Add parentheses liberally to document precedence and intention, even when not strictly required
Write and run tests around boundary values (e.g., 0, 1, -1, exact thresholds) to catch off-by-one and ordering bugs
Prefer early returns (guard clauses) to reduce nesting and make exceptional cases obvious

vocabulary.definition: A boolean expression that controls program flow by evaluating to true or false.
A data type with two values: true and false.
Operators that compare numeric values: <, <=, >, >=.
Operators that test primitive equality or inequality: == and !=.
Operators that combine booleans: && (and), || (or), ! (not).
Evaluation strategy where && and || stop early if the result is already determined.
Executes a block only when a condition is true.
Chooses between two blocks based on a condition.
A sequence of conditions to select among multiple exclusive cases.
An if statement inside another if/else block.
Multi-way selection based on a single expression’s value.
A concise conditional expression: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse.
An early return that exits a method when a precondition fails.
Comparing String contents with equals or equalsIgnoreCase, not ==.
Rules for negating compound conditions: !(A || B) == (!A && !B), !(A && B) == (!A || !B).

quiz.question: What is the result of: true || false && false?
quiz.explanation: && has higher precedence than ||, so false && false is false, then true || false is true.

quiz.question: Given: int x = 10, y = 3; if (x > 5) if (y > 5) System.out.println("A"); else System.out.println("B"); What prints?
quiz.explanation: The else pairs with the nearest unmatched if (y > 5). Since y > 5 is false, the else branch prints B.

quiz.question: How do you correctly test two Strings s and t for content equality?
quiz.explanation: Use equals to compare the contents of String objects. == compares references.

quiz.question: What does this print? int n = 0; if (n > 0) System.out.print("pos"); else if (n == 0) System.out.print("zero"); else System.out.print("neg");
quiz.explanation: Since n == 0, the else-if branch executes.

quiz.question: String s = null; boolean b = (s != null && s.length() > 0); What happens?
options: A) b is false; no exception is thrown
quiz.explanation: Short-circuiting prevents s.length() from running when s != null is false, so b becomes false safely.

quiz.question: Which is logically equivalent to !(x > 10 || y == 0)?
quiz.explanation: Apply De Morgan's law: !(A || B) == (!A && !B). ! (x > 10) is x <= 10; ! (y == 0) is y != 0.

quiz.question: String label = (7 % 2 == 0) ? "even" : "odd"; What is label?
quiz.explanation: 7 is not divisible by 2, so the false branch "odd" is chosen.

quiz.question: char g = 'B'; switch (g) { case 'A': case 'B': System.out.print("pass"); break; default: System.out.print("other"); } What prints?
quiz.explanation: Cases 'A' and 'B' share the same print before break; for 'B' it prints pass.

quiz.question: Which is the best way to check if doubles a and b are approximately equal?
quiz.explanation: Floating-point rounding makes exact equality unreliable; use an epsilon tolerance.

quiz.question: What is wrong with: if (x = 5) { ... }
options: A) Use == for comparison; = is assignment
D) Nothing; it assigns 5 and tests it
quiz.explanation: Java requires a boolean in an if condition; x = 5 assigns an int and causes a compile-time type error. Use x == 5.

INPUT temp

    PRINT "Freezing"
ELSE IF temp < 10
    PRINT "Cold"
ELSE IF temp < 25
    PRINT "Mild"
ELSE IF temp < 35

pseudocodeExamples.title: Guard clause for safe division
pseudocodeExamples.description: Illustrates early return to handle invalid input before main logic, preventing divide-by-zero.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION safeDivide(a, b)
    IF b == 0
        PRINT "Cannot divide by zero"
        RETURN 0
    END IF
    RETURN a / b
END FUNCTION

syntaxReference: If: if (condition) { /* then */ }
If-else: if (cond) { ... } else { ... }
Else-if chain: if (a) { ... } else if (b) { ... } else { ... }
Nested ifs: if (a) { if (b) { ... } } // use braces to avoid dangling else
Logical operators: !a, a && b, a || b // precedence: ! > && > ||
String equality: if (s != null && s.equals("hi")) { ... }
Ternary: String msg = ok ? "yes" : "no";
Switch: switch(x) { case 1: ...; break; case 2: ...; break; default: ...; }
commonPatterns: Range check: if (0 <= x && x <= 100) { ... }
Mutually exclusive buckets: if (score >= 90) {...} else if (score >= 80) {...} ... else {...}
Null guard: if (obj == null) return; // or: if (obj != null && obj.method())...
Safe String test: if (s != null && !s.isEmpty()) { ... }
De Morgan rewrite: if (!(a || b)) -> if (!a && !b)
quickTips: Prefer clear parentheses to relying on operator precedence.
Test boundary values around each comparison to catch off-by-one errors.
Minimize nesting with early returns (guard clauses) for invalid or special cases.

topics.overview:
Strings in Java represent immutable sequences of Unicode characters and are one of the most-used data types in CS106A. You will use strings to read user input, parse files, build messages, and implement algorithms that process text. Because strings are objects, they come with a rich set of methods for inspection (length, charAt), searching (indexOf, lastIndexOf), slicing (substring), and transforming (toUpperCase, toLowerCase, trim, replace).

A key idea is immutability: once created, a String's contents never change. Methods that seem like they "modify" a string actually return a new one. This affects performance and correctness—avoid repeatedly concatenating with + inside loops; prefer StringBuilder for efficient construction. Other core practices include using equals (not ==) for content comparison, remembering that substring end indexes are exclusive, and handling "not found" results from indexOf as -1.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
• Strings are objects: variables hold references to String instances created via literals ("...") or constructors. Strings store Unicode text and are zero-indexed.
• Immutability: all String methods that "change" text return a new String. Original references keep their original value. This has implications for memory and performance, especially in loops.
• Core operations: length() gives character count; charAt(i) retrieves a char; substring(start, end) returns a half-open slice [start, end); indexOf/lastIndexOf search for substrings and return -1 when not found; equals and equalsIgnoreCase compare content; compareTo provides lexicographic ordering; trim removes leading/trailing whitespace; replace replaces characters/substrings; case conversion via toUpperCase/toLowerCase.
• Concatenation: + can join strings and other types; evaluation is left-to-right. Be mindful of numeric addition vs concatenation order. String.format provides powerful, readable templating.
• Efficiency: concatenating inside a loop can be O(n^2). Use StringBuilder (append, toString) to build large strings efficiently. Choose the right tool for readability and performance.
learningObjectives: Use String methods (length, charAt, substring, indexOf) to inspect, slice, and search text
Compare strings correctly with equals and equalsIgnoreCase and explain why == is wrong for content
Apply substring’s half-open indexing and avoid off-by-one errors in loops over characters
Choose between +, String.format, and StringBuilder based on readability and performance needs
Implement small text-processing tasks (counting, parsing, normalization) using string APIs
commonMistakes: Using == instead of equals to compare two strings, leading to incorrect results
Forgetting substring’s end index is exclusive, causing off-by-one bugs or StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
Building long strings with + inside a loop instead of using StringBuilder, causing poor performance
tipsForSuccess: Write small helper methods (e.g., isVowel, normalize) to keep string logic readable and testable
Check edge cases: empty strings, single-character strings, and not-found cases (indexOf returns -1)
Print intermediate results while debugging to verify indexes and substrings are what you expect

vocabulary.definition: Text enclosed in double quotes that creates a String instance at compile time.
Joining strings (and other values) into a single string; evaluated left-to-right.
Returns the number of char units in the string.
Returns the char at a zero-based index.
Returns the half-open slice [start, end); end is exclusive.
Returns the first index where s occurs, or -1 if not found.
Returns the last index where s occurs, or -1 if not found.
Checks content equality; use for comparing string values.
Checks content equality ignoring letter case.
Lexicographically compares two strings; negative, zero, or positive.
Return new strings with all letters converted to upper/lower case.
Removes leading and trailing whitespace.
Returns a new string with all occurrences of old replaced by new.
A mutable sequence of characters for efficient appending and editing.
vocabulary.example: StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); sb.append("hi"); String s = sb.toString();

vocabulary.definition: Creates formatted strings using printf-style placeholders.

quiz.question: Which expression correctly returns the number of characters in String s?
quiz.explanation: length() is the method on String; length (no parentheses) is for arrays, not strings.

quiz.question: How do you correctly test if two strings s1 and s2 have the same contents?
quiz.explanation: Use equals to compare string content; == tests reference identity.

quiz.question: What is printed? String s = "Stanford"; System.out.println(s.substring(2, 6));
quiz.explanation: substring is half-open: indices 2..5 are 'a','n','f','o' → "anfo".

quiz.question: What does indexOf return if the substring is not found?
quiz.explanation: indexOf returns -1 when the query substring is absent.

quiz.question: What is printed? String s = "Hi"; s.toLowerCase(); System.out.println(s);
quiz.explanation: Strings are immutable. toLowerCase returns a new string; s is unchanged.

quiz.question: What is printed? String s = "cs"; System.out.println(1 + 2 + s + 3 + 4);
quiz.explanation: Left-to-right: 1+2=3; then "3cs"; then "3cs3"; then "3cs34".

quiz.question: When building a very long string in a loop, which is most efficient?
options: C) Use StringBuilder and call append
D) Convert to char[] and back each time
quiz.explanation: StringBuilder is mutable and avoids creating many temporary strings.

quiz.question: What is the sign of "Apple".compareTo("Banana")?
quiz.explanation: "Apple" comes before "Banana" lexicographically, so the result is < 0.

quiz.question: What is the result of "banana".lastIndexOf("an")?
quiz.explanation: Occurrences at indices 1 and 3; lastIndexOf returns the latter: 3.

quiz.question: What is the value of String code = String.format("%s-%03d", "CS", 6);
quiz.explanation: %03d pads the integer with zeros to width 3, yielding "CS-006".

pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
function sameEmail(a, b):
  if a is null or b is null: return false
  set na = lowercase(trim(a))
  set nb = lowercase(trim(b))

pseudocodeExamples.title: Find Longest Word in a Sentence
pseudocodeExamples.description: Splits on whitespace, cleans punctuation, and tracks the longest token.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
function longestWord(sentence):
  if sentence is null or empty: return ""
  words = split(trim(sentence)) on whitespace
  longest = ""
  for each w in words:
    clean = remove non-letter chars from w
    if length(clean) > length(longest): longest = clean
  return longest

Concatenate: String msg = "CS" + 106 + "A";
Length: int n = s.length();

Compare: boolean eq = s.equals(t); boolean ci = s.equalsIgnoreCase(t);
Ordering: int cmp = s.compareTo(t); // <0, 0, >0
Trim/case: s.trim(); s.toUpperCase(); s.toLowerCase();
Replace: String r = s.replace("-", ":");
Formatting: String out = String.format("%s-%03d", "CS", 6);
StringBuilder: StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); sb.append(x); String res = sb.toString();

Split on whitespace:
String[] tokens = s.trim().split("\\s+");

Safe numeric concat:

Remember substring end index is exclusive; watch for off-by-one errors.
Prefer StringBuilder for heavy concatenation inside loops.

topics.overview:
In CS106A, Arrays and ArrayLists are your primary tools for managing collections of values. Arrays provide fast, indexed access with a fixed size, making them efficient and predictable for tasks like processing sensor readings, image pixels, or characters in a string. You’ll learn to declare, initialize, index, iterate, and perform common operations such as searching, aggregating, and copying. You’ll also encounter multidimensional arrays (e.g., 2D arrays) to represent grids, boards, and matrices.

ArrayList is a resizable, object-based collection from Java’s standard library. It trades a bit of overhead for flexibility: you can add, remove, and insert elements dynamically without managing capacity manually. In CS106A you’ll practice when to choose arrays vs. ArrayList, how generics and autoboxing work (e.g., ArrayList<Integer>), how to iterate safely (for-each, indices, iterators), and how to avoid common pitfalls like off-by-one errors, aliasing instead of copying, and modifying lists during iteration.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
Arrays are fixed-size, contiguous collections that store primitives or object references; they support O(1) indexed access via zero-based indices and expose their size through the length field. They can be single- or multi-dimensional (e.g., int[][] for grids), and elements default to type-appropriate values (0 for numeric primitives, false for boolean, null for object references). Common operations include traversal (for and enhanced for), searching, aggregation (sum, min/max), copying (Arrays.copyOf, clone, System.arraycopy), and sorting (Arrays.sort).

ArrayList<E> is a dynamic, resizable array-backed list for objects. You specify the element type via generics (e.g., ArrayList<String>), and interact through methods like add, get, set, remove, size, contains, and indexOf. It cannot store primitives directly; use wrapper types (Integer, Double) with autoboxing/unboxing. Iteration can use indices, enhanced for, listIterator/iterator, or higher-level operations like removeIf. Watch out for ConcurrentModificationException when removing during enhanced for, and understand the difference between logical size (size()) and capacity. Choose arrays for fixed-size, performance-critical tasks and ArrayList for flexible, dynamic collections.
learningObjectives: Declare, initialize, and index into 1D and 2D arrays; use length and nested loops effectively.
Use ArrayList with generics to add, access, modify, and remove elements; differentiate size() from capacity.
Select appropriate iteration strategies (indexed for, enhanced for, iterator) and know when each is safe to modify.
Avoid common pitfalls: off-by-one errors, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, aliasing vs copying, and modification during iteration.
Convert between arrays and ArrayLists; apply utility methods (Arrays.sort, Arrays.equals, Collections.sort, removeIf).
commonMistakes: Using arr[arr.length] or negative indices, causing ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
Confusing array length with ArrayList size() or expecting ArrayList to hold primitives directly.
Aliasing arrays (int[] b = a) when a true copy is intended, leading to unintended side effects.
tipsForSuccess: Draw memory diagrams for arrays and ArrayLists to reason about references, aliasing, and null values.
Write small helper methods (e.g., max, copy, toList) and unit-test them to localize bugs like off-by-one errors.
Prefer enhanced for for read-only traversal; use indices or iterators when you need to modify during traversal.

quiz.question: Which declaration creates an int array of size 5?
quiz.explanation: Arrays are declared with type[], and new int[5] allocates space for 5 ints. The other options are invalid or create a size-1 array with a single element 5.

quiz.question: Given int[] a = {1, 2, 3}; how do you access the last element?
quiz.explanation: Valid indices are 0..a.length-1; the last element is at index a.length - 1.

quiz.question: Which statement about size is correct?
options: C) Arrays use length; ArrayList uses size()
D) ArrayList has capacity() for element count
quiz.explanation: Arrays expose a length field; ArrayList exposes a size() method. There is no length on ArrayList.

quiz.question: What happens with: for (Integer x : list) { if (x % 2 == 0) list.remove(x); }
options: A) It safely removes all even numbers.
D) It silently skips some elements but succeeds.
quiz.explanation: Modifying an ArrayList during an enhanced for triggers ConcurrentModificationException. Use an Iterator with remove() or list.removeIf(...).

quiz.question: With ArrayList<Integer> list, what does list.add(5) do?
options: A) Compile error: must write new Integer(5).
B) Stores primitive int directly in the list.
C) Autoboxes to Integer(5) and adds it.
D) Converts list to int[] and appends 5.
quiz.explanation: Autoboxing converts the primitive 5 to an Integer object automatically.

quiz.question: Given int[] a = {1,2,3}; which statement aliases the same underlying array rather than copying it?
quiz.explanation: Assigning b = a makes b refer to the same array. The others create or perform copies (clone/copyOf/System.arraycopy).

quiz.question: What are default initial values for new int[3] and new String[3]?
options: A) Uninitialized; compile error on access.
quiz.explanation: Numeric primitives default to 0; object references default to null.

quiz.question: Which code correctly removes all even numbers from ArrayList<Integer> list?
options: A) for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) if (list.get(i) % 2 == 0) list.remove(i);
B) for (Integer x : list) if (x % 2 == 0) list.remove(x);
quiz.explanation: removeIf safely removes elements matching a predicate. A) skips some elements; B) throws ConcurrentModificationException; D) is not a valid API.

quiz.question: Given int[][] m = new int[3][4]; which expression is the number of rows?
quiz.explanation: For 2D arrays, outer length is the number of rows; inner length (m[0].length) is columns.

quiz.question: How do you sort an ArrayList<String> names in ascending order?
quiz.explanation: Collections.sort(list) sorts a List in natural order. Arrays.sort is for arrays, and names.sort() requires an argument or Java 8 method reference but is not used as shown.

Find index of target in array: int idx = -1; for (int i = 0; i < a.length; i++) { if (a[i] == target) { idx = i; break; } }

topics.overview:
In CS106A, Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is the organizing principle that helps you structure Java programs around objects that contain both data (state) and behavior (methods). Instead of writing one long script, you design classes that model concepts from your problem domain—like BankAccount, Student, or Shape—and then create instances (objects) that collaborate to solve the task. This approach scales from small assignments to large software systems because it promotes modularity, readability, and reuse.

You will practice key OOP ideas such as encapsulation (hiding internal details behind a clear public interface), constructors (initializing object state), and method design. You’ll also get an introduction to inheritance, interfaces, and polymorphism to write general code that works across different types. By the end, you’ll be able to design clean, testable classes, use standard patterns, and reason about object interactions—skills that carry across languages and projects.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
OOP in Java centers on classes (blueprints) and objects (instances). A class defines fields (instance variables) to hold state and methods to provide behavior. Constructors initialize new objects and can enforce invariants. Encapsulation keeps fields private and exposes behavior through a small, well-documented public interface, which helps prevent bugs and makes changes safer.

Access modifiers (public, private, protected) control visibility. The keyword this refers to the current object, useful for disambiguating fields from parameters. Static members belong to the class itself rather than any instance (e.g., utility functions or shared constants). Inheritance lets a subclass reuse and extend a superclass; polymorphism enables code that uses a superclass or interface reference to operate on any subclass/implementer. Method overriding customizes behavior, and super can invoke superclass constructors or methods. Interfaces define contracts of behavior without implementation, enabling flexible design and decoupling. Together, these features help you build modular, reusable, and maintainable programs.
learningObjectives: Design and implement Java classes with fields, constructors, and methods that enforce class invariants.
Apply encapsulation using appropriate access modifiers, getters/setters, and clear public interfaces.
Differentiate between instance and static members and use them appropriately.
Use inheritance, interfaces, and polymorphism to write general, extensible code.
Override toString, equals, and other methods to integrate with Java conventions and collections.
commonMistakes: Exposing fields as public instead of keeping them private and validating via methods.
Using == to compare Strings or objects rather than equals, leading to incorrect comparisons.
Forgetting to call super(...) in subclass constructors or misunderstanding constructor chaining.
tipsForSuccess: Write small, focused classes with a single responsibility; test behavior via public methods.
Document class invariants and method contracts; enforce them in constructors and setters.
Prefer composition over inheritance when the relationship is "has-a" rather than "is-a".

vocabulary.definition: A blueprint that defines the structure and behavior of objects.

Reference to the current object; used to access fields/methods or chain constructors.
Belongs to the class, not instances; shared across all objects.

Different classes can be treated uniformly through a common type; method calls dispatch to the actual type.
A contract specifying methods a class must implement, without implementations.
A class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods.

quiz.question: Which statement best describes encapsulation?
options: A) Storing multiple objects in an array.
B) Hiding internal state and exposing behavior through a public interface.
C) Allowing a class to inherit from multiple superclasses.
D) Compiling code into bytecode for the JVM.
quiz.explanation: Encapsulation hides fields and exposes behavior via methods, protecting invariants and reducing coupling.

quiz.question: What is the primary difference between a class and an object?
options: A) A class runs code, while an object defines code.
B) A class is a blueprint; an object is an instance created from that blueprint.
C) A class stores data; an object stores methods.
D) There is no difference; they are synonyms.
quiz.explanation: A class defines structure/behavior; an object is a concrete instance with its own state.

quiz.question: Given String a = new String("hi"); String b = "hi"; which compares content correctly?
quiz.explanation: equals compares String content; == compares references.

quiz.question: Which keyword is used in a subclass to invoke its superclass constructor?
quiz.explanation: super(...) must be the first statement to call a superclass constructor.

quiz.question: What happens when a subclass overrides a method of its superclass?
options: A) The superclass method is permanently deleted.
B) At runtime, the method of the actual object type is called (dynamic dispatch).
C) The subclass cannot access any superclass fields.
D) The subclass must be abstract.
quiz.explanation: Polymorphism ensures the most specific override is dispatched at runtime based on the object’s concrete type.

quiz.question: Which is true about static members?
options: A) Static fields are copied into every object.
B) Static methods can directly access instance fields.
C) Static members belong to the class, not any instance.
D) Static members are only available in interfaces.
quiz.explanation: Static members are class-level; there is a single copy per class and they are accessed via ClassName.member.

quiz.question: Which declaration correctly defines an interface in Java?
options: A) abstract interface Runnable { void run() {} }
C) class Runnable implements Interface { void run(); }
D) interface Runnable() { public void run(); }
quiz.explanation: Interfaces declare method signatures without bodies (unless default/static).

quiz.question: Consider: class A { void f() {} } class B extends A { void f() {} } A x = new B(); Which f is called by x.f()?
options: A) A.f because x is declared as A
B) B.f because the object is a B
C) Neither; this is a compile error
quiz.explanation: Dynamic dispatch calls the method of the object’s actual type (B), not the reference type.

quiz.question: Which design choice best indicates composition over inheritance?
options: C) class Car { private Engine engine; }
quiz.explanation: A car has-an engine, so composition (a field) is preferred over is-a (inheritance).

quiz.question: Which equals implementation is most correct?
options: A) public boolean equals(Object o) { return this == o; }
B) public boolean equals(Point p) { return x == p.x && y == p.y; }
C) public boolean equals(Object o) { if (!(o instanceof Point)) return false; Point p = (Point)o; return x == p.x && y == p.y; }
D) public boolean equals() { return true; }
quiz.explanation: equals must override the Object signature and handle non-Point inputs safely.

topics.overview:
In CS106A, Graphics and Animation introduce you to event-driven programming and visual problem solving using the ACM Graphics library. You work in a GraphicsProgram, add graphical objects (GObject) like GRect, GOval, and GLabel to a canvas, set their colors and layering, and position them using a 2D coordinate system with the origin at the top-left. You’ll also learn to load images, group shapes, and display text to create interactive visual programs.

Animation is built around a simple game loop: update object state (e.g., position), render, and pause for a short time step to control frame rate. Combining this with mouse and keyboard listeners lets you build interactive projects like bouncing balls, drag-and-drop interfaces, simple arcade games (Breakout), and data visualizations. You’ll practice timing, collision detection, and incremental design to keep programs smooth, responsive, and easy to debug.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
GraphicsProgram provides a drawing canvas where you add and manipulate GObject instances (e.g., GRect, GOval, GLine, GImage, GLabel). The coordinate system has (0,0) at the top-left; x increases to the right, y increases downward. You can place objects with add(obj, x, y), move them using setLocation or move(dx, dy), and control appearance with setColor, setFilled, and setFillColor. Z-order determines which objects appear on top; you can adjust it with sendToFront/sendToBack.

Animation in CS106A typically uses a loop with small movement steps and pause(milliseconds) to throttle speed and yield to the event system. Velocity variables (dx, dy) encode motion; you update position then reflect or clamp when hitting boundaries. Collision detection ranges from point sampling (e.g., getElementAt(x,y)) to bounding-box checks via getBounds() on GObject and GRectangle intersection tests. Interactivity is handled through mouse and keyboard listeners: addMouseListeners/addKeyListeners plus methods like mousePressed, mouseDragged, and keyPressed.

Images (GImage) and groups (GCompound) enable richer scenes and modular composition: build complex sprites by combining primitives into a single movable unit. Robust programs separate constants (sizes, speeds) from logic, and follow an incremental workflow—draw, move, then handle collisions and input—while using debugging aids like outlines, labels, and color changes.
learningObjectives: Create a GraphicsProgram and add, position, and style shapes, text, and images on the canvas.
Animate objects using a time-stepped loop with pause, managing velocities and frame rates.
Implement collision detection using point sampling, object bounds, and simple reflection/bounce logic.
Handle mouse and keyboard events to enable dragging, clicking, and character or paddle control.
Compose scenes with GCompound and manage layering (z-order) for correct drawing and interaction.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call setFilled(true) when expecting a shape’s fill color to appear (setColor only affects outlines unless filled).
Using canvas dimensions (getWidth/getHeight) or object dimensions incorrectly in boundary checks, causing objects to clip or escape the window.
Not calling addMouseListeners/addKeyListeners (or not overriding the correct handler names), resulting in unresponsive input.
tipsForSuccess: Use named constants for sizes, speeds, and timings; this makes tuning animations easier and safer.
Develop incrementally: first draw static objects, then add motion, then add collisions and input, testing after each step.
Visualize debugging info: temporarily draw hitboxes, change colors on collisions, or print positions to track logic errors.

A group of GObjects that move together as one.

vocabulary.definition: Controls whether a shape is filled and its fill color.
Shifts an object by (dx, dy) relative to its current position.
Places an object at an absolute (x, y) position.
Delays the program for a number of milliseconds, enabling smooth animation.
Returns the topmost GObject at a canvas point, or null if none.
The drawing order (front-to-back layering) of objects on the canvas.
Mouse and keyboard handlers that respond to user input.

quiz.question: Where is the origin (0,0) on the GraphicsProgram canvas?
quiz.explanation: ACM Graphics uses a screen coordinate system with (0,0) at the top-left; x increases right, y increases downward.

quiz.question: Which call properly places a GRect r at coordinates (100, 200)?
quiz.explanation: You can place while adding using add(r, x, y). setLocation is fine too, but only after the object is added; option A alone does not add the object.

quiz.question: What does pause(16) in an animation loop most directly control?
quiz.explanation: pause(16) delays about 16 ms per frame, targeting roughly 60 frames per second.

quiz.question: Which method moves a GObject relative to its current position?
quiz.explanation: move(dx, dy) offsets the current position by the given deltas.

quiz.question: To bounce a ball off the right wall, which condition is most appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Check the ball’s right edge: x + width. When it reaches or exceeds the canvas width, reverse dx.

quiz.question: Which call ensures a rectangle’s interior shows the chosen color?
quiz.explanation: You must mark the shape as filled and set its fill color; setColor only changes the outline if not filled.

quiz.question: What is the best way to detect an object directly under a point (x, y)?
options: C) contains(x, y) on every object manually
quiz.explanation: getElementAt(x, y) queries the canvas and returns the topmost GObject at that location.

quiz.question: You wrote an infinite while loop without pause(). What is the most likely symptom?
options: C) The UI becomes unresponsive and animation runs too fast to see
quiz.explanation: Without pause, the loop monopolizes CPU and starves event handling and repainting, freezing the UI.

quiz.question: Which method changes the layering so an object appears above others?
quiz.explanation: sendToFront() places the GObject at the front of the z-order.

quiz.question: You want arrow keys to move a player. What must be done first?
options: A) Call addKeyListeners() and override keyPressed(KeyEvent e)
quiz.explanation: Register for keyboard events with addKeyListeners() and implement keyPressed/keyReleased to handle input.

syntaxReference: Program skeleton: public class MySketch extends GraphicsProgram { public void run() { /* ... */ } }
Add objects: GRect r = new GRect(80,40); add(r, 100, 120);
Colors and fill: rect.setFilled(true); rect.setFillColor(Color.BLUE); rect.setColor(Color.BLACK);
Movement: obj.move(dx, dy); obj.setLocation(x, y);

topics.overview:
Event-driven programming is a style where your program reacts to events—user actions like clicks and key presses, or time-based triggers—rather than running a single top-to-bottom script. In CS106A’s Java context, you’ll use the ACM libraries (GraphicsProgram and interactors) to register listeners that respond to specific events. The framework runs an event loop behind the scenes, places incoming events on a queue, and calls your handler methods (callbacks) when those events occur.

Instead of polling for input, you write small, focused methods such as actionPerformed, mousePressed, mouseDragged, and keyPressed that update state and the display. This encourages a clean separation of concerns: events capture user intent, handlers update the model (program state), and the canvas (GCanvas) visually reflects those changes via GObjects. Timers add time-based events for animation, enabling smooth motion without blocking the user interface.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
At the core of event-driven programming are events, listeners, and callbacks. Events represent occurrences (button clicks, mouse moves, key presses, or timer ticks). Listeners are objects (often your GraphicsProgram) that subscribe to receive notifications about those events. Callbacks are the methods invoked by the framework when an event happens. In CS106A with the ACM libraries, you register for events using helper methods such as addActionListeners(), addMouseListeners(), and addKeyListeners(). Interactors (like JButton and JTextField) and graphical objects (GObject, GOval, GRect) connect user input to program behavior.

Handlers should be short and state-based. They read from the event object (e.g., coordinates from MouseEvent, command strings from ActionEvent), update program state, and request or cause a redraw (moving GObjects, changing labels). Timers (javax.swing.Timer) generate periodic ActionEvents for animation and repeated tasks, keeping the UI responsive. Best practices include separating model from view, storing minimal persistent state (like selected objects and drag offsets), and discriminating among multiple event sources via e.getSource() or e.getActionCommand().
learningObjectives: Register and handle UI events (action, mouse, keyboard) in a GraphicsProgram
Use ActionEvent, MouseEvent, and KeyEvent objects to read user input details
Implement drag-and-drop behavior and hit-testing with getElementAt(x, y)
Animate graphics using javax.swing.Timer without blocking the UI
Maintain clean state to coordinate event handlers and rendering
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call addActionListeners(), addMouseListeners(), or addKeyListeners(), resulting in handlers that never run
Comparing strings with == instead of .equals(), causing action command checks to fail
Doing long-running work inside handlers, freezing the UI and missing subsequent events
tipsForSuccess: Keep handlers short: update state, adjust visuals, and return quickly
Track the minimum state needed (e.g., selected GObject and drag offsets) to make handlers simple
Use a Timer for smooth, consistent animation instead of while loops with pause

topics.overview:
In CS106A, the memory model explains where and how your Java programs store data while they run. The two main regions are the stack and the heap. The stack holds method call frames, which include local variables and parameters. The heap stores objects and arrays created with new. Understanding the relationship between these regions clarifies why some changes inside methods affect the caller and others do not.

A crucial part of the model is the difference between primitives and references. Primitives (int, double, boolean, char, etc.) store actual values directly in stack frames. Variables that name objects or arrays store references—pointers to heap locations. Passing arguments in Java is always pass-by-value: for primitives, the value is copied; for objects, the reference is copied, so methods can mutate the same object but cannot rebind the caller’s variable. Concepts like aliasing, null, immutability (e.g., Strings), equality (== vs equals), and shallow vs deep copying all fit into this picture and help you write correct, bug-free code.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
Java organizes runtime memory into the stack and the heap. Each method call pushes a stack frame containing its parameters and local variables; returning from a method pops that frame. Objects and arrays live on the heap and are accessed via references stored in variables. Primitives store raw values; reference variables store addresses that point to heap objects.

Java uses pass-by-value for all parameters. When passing a primitive, the numeric/boolean/char value is copied; reassigning the parameter does not affect the caller. When passing an object, the reference is copied; both the parameter and caller’s variable point to the same heap object, so mutating fields through the parameter is visible to the caller. However, reassigning the parameter to a different object only changes the local copy of the reference. Aliasing occurs when multiple references point to the same heap object; changes through one alias are seen through the others. Strings are immutable, so operations like concat create new objects rather than changing existing ones. Equality with == compares references (identity) for objects, while equals typically compares content. Copying structures requires care: shallow copies duplicate references, while deep copies recursively duplicate data. Unreferenced heap objects are reclaimed by garbage collection.
learningObjectives: Differentiate stack vs heap roles during program execution.
Explain Java pass-by-value semantics for primitives and references.
Predict effects of aliasing on arrays and objects after assignments and method calls.
Choose between == and equals when comparing objects, especially Strings.
Create shallow or deep copies appropriately to avoid unintended sharing.
commonMistakes: Using == to compare String contents instead of equals, leading to incorrect comparisons.
Assuming Java is pass-by-reference for objects and expecting a method to reassign the caller’s variable.
Accidentally aliasing arrays/objects (e.g., b = a) and then being surprised when changes to one affect the other.
tipsForSuccess: Draw memory diagrams (stack frames and heap objects) to trace code behavior.
Adopt defensive copying when sharing mutable arrays or objects across methods.
Use final for references you do not intend to rebind and prefer immutable data when possible.

vocabulary.definition: Region storing method call frames with parameters and local variables.
vocabulary.example: void f() { int x = 10; /* x lives in f's stack frame */ }

vocabulary.definition: Region storing objects and arrays created with new.
vocabulary.example: int[] a = new int[3]; // 'a' references an array on the heap

vocabulary.definition: Activation record for a method call; holds locals, parameters, and return address.
vocabulary.example: main calls sum(3,4) -> a new frame for sum is pushed on the stack

vocabulary.definition: Built-in type that stores a value directly (not a reference).
vocabulary.example: int n = 42; double d = 3.14; boolean ok = true;

vocabulary.definition: A value that points to a heap object or array.
vocabulary.example: Point p = new Point(1, 2); // p stores a reference to a Point

vocabulary.definition: A heap-allocated instance containing fields and methods defined by a class.
vocabulary.example: String s = new String("hi"); // s references a String object

vocabulary.definition: Two or more references pointing to the same heap object.
Java copies argument values into parameters; object parameters get a copy of the reference.
vocabulary.example: void setX(Point p){ p.x = 5; } // mutates same object seen by caller

vocabulary.definition: A reference that points to no object; using it causes NullPointerException on dereference.
vocabulary.example: String s = null; // s.length() would throw NullPointerException

vocabulary.definition: Automatic reclamation of heap objects no longer reachable by any reference.
vocabulary.example: p = new Point(); p = null; // old Point becomes eligible for GC

vocabulary.definition: Property where an object’s state cannot change after creation.
vocabulary.example: String t = "hi"; t.concat("!"); // t unchanged; result is a new String

vocabulary.definition: Object state can change after creation.
vocabulary.example: Point p = new Point(0,0); p.x = 10; // p mutated

vocabulary.definition: Identity (==) compares references; equals compares content (if implemented).
vocabulary.example: new String("a") == new String("a") // false; equals returns true

vocabulary.definition: Copy that duplicates references, not the nested objects themselves.
vocabulary.example: int[][] b = a.clone(); // top-level array cloned; inner arrays shared

vocabulary.definition: Copy that recursively duplicates all contained mutable data.

quiz.question: Where are local variables of a method stored during its execution?
quiz.explanation: Each method call creates a stack frame that holds its locals and parameters.

quiz.question: What does a Java reference variable actually store?
options: A) The object’s full data inline
B) A copy of the object’s fields
C) An address/pointer to a heap object
D) The class definition of the object
quiz.explanation: References are pointers to heap objects; the object data lives on the heap.

quiz.question: Given int[] a = {1,2}; int[] b = a; b[0] = 9;, what is a[0]?
quiz.explanation: a and b alias the same array; modifying b modifies the same heap array.

quiz.question: Java parameter passing is best described as:
options: B) Pass-by-value for primitives, pass-by-reference for objects
quiz.explanation: Java copies the value; for objects, the value copied is the reference.

quiz.question: Consider void foo(Point p){ p = new Point(5,5); }. If Point q = new Point(0,0); foo(q); what is q.x after the call?
quiz.explanation: Reassigning p only changes the local copy of the reference; q still points to (0,0).

quiz.question: Which comparison checks whether two String variables have the same sequence of characters?
quiz.explanation: equals compares content; == checks reference identity for objects.

quiz.question: Which statement about Strings is true?
options: A) Strings are mutable; concat changes the original
B) Strings are immutable; concat returns a new String
C) Strings live on the stack
D) Strings cannot be passed to methods
quiz.explanation: Strings are immutable; operations produce new objects on the heap.

quiz.question: When does a heap object become eligible for garbage collection?
options: B) When it goes out of scope
C) When no reachable reference points to it
D) When the program ends a method
quiz.explanation: Eligibility occurs when the object is unreachable from any live reference chain.

quiz.question: What is the primary risk of shallow copying a structure containing mutable objects?
options: C) Unintended aliasing and shared mutation
quiz.explanation: Shallow copies reuse references, so changes via one reference appear in the other.

quiz.question: Given int[] a = {1,2,3}; int[] b = Arrays.copyOf(a, a.length); b[1] = 99;, what is a[1]?
quiz.explanation: Arrays.copyOf creates a new array; no aliasing, so a is unchanged.

pseudocodeExamples.description: Highlights the difference between == and equals when comparing Strings.

make s1 = new String("hi")
make s2 = new String("hi")
identity check (s1 == s2) is false
content check (s1.equals(s2)) is true

Declare references: Point p; p = new Point(0,0);
Array creation: int[] a = new int[5]; int[] b = {1,2,3};

Prefer immutable data (e.g., Strings) to reduce aliasing bugs.

topics.overview:
In CS106A, File I/O (input/output) lets your programs work with persistent data instead of just the console. You read information from files (like data sets, configuration, or logs) and write results back to new files for later use or sharing. This is essential for tasks such as analyzing datasets, transforming text, or saving the state of a program.

You will use Java classes such as Scanner, BufferedReader, PrintWriter, and Files to read and write text files efficiently. Along the way you will manage file paths, handle exceptions (e.g., FileNotFoundException, IOException), use try-with-resources to close files automatically, and parse structured text (lines, tokens, CSV/TSV). By the end, you should be comfortable building programs that load data from disk, process it, and save results safely and predictably.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
File I/O centers on streams that move data between your program and storage. For text files, you typically use readers/writers (character streams) such as BufferedReader and PrintWriter or convenience classes like Scanner that tokenize input. Buffering improves performance by reducing the number of operating system calls, so a common pattern is new BufferedReader(new FileReader(...)). The java.nio.file.Files and Paths utilities provide concise, modern APIs for common tasks such as reading all lines, walking directories, and creating writers/readers.

Paths can be absolute or relative; relative paths are resolved against the program’s working directory. Since I/O can fail (missing files, permission errors, encoding issues), Java requires you to handle checked exceptions like IOException. The try-with-resources construct ensures files are closed even if errors occur, preventing resource leaks and ensuring data is flushed to disk. Parsing strategies include reading line-by-line and splitting, or using Scanner with custom delimiters for token-based processing. Choosing the right approach depends on data structure, memory constraints, and performance needs.
learningObjectives: Open, read, and write text files using Scanner, BufferedReader, PrintWriter, and Files
Use try-with-resources to manage file handles and guarantee closure
Choose appropriate parsing strategies (line-based vs token-based) for structured text
Handle file-related exceptions and report errors gracefully to users
Work with paths (relative/absolute) and understand the program’s working directory
commonMistakes: Forgetting to close writers/readers, leading to unflushed output or file handle leaks
Using the wrong method for parsing (e.g., mixing next() and nextLine() incorrectly) causing skipped input
Assuming relative paths point to the project folder when the working directory differs
tipsForSuccess: Start with small test files and print intermediate results to verify parsing logic
Encapsulate file operations in helper methods that clearly document the format they expect
Prefer try-with-resources and specify character encodings (e.g., UTF-8) to avoid subtle bugs

vocabulary.definition: An object representing a file system path (location) to a file or directory.

Utility class (java.nio.file) with static methods for common file operations.

vocabulary.example: new Scanner(new File("missing.txt")); // may throw FileNotFoundException

vocabulary.definition: A character or pattern that separates tokens when parsing text.
A common Unicode text encoding; recommended default for cross-platform text files.
